briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now begin the climate briefing for May 19, 2026.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Strengthening Domestic Climate Crisis Response and Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Today, various efforts to respond to the climate crisis were reported domestically. Busan City has taken proactive measures by strengthening its algal bloom warning system as the risk of green algae increases due to climate change [2, 5, 6], while future generations and farmers have clearly stated their perception of the climate crisis as an issue of survival rights, demanding 'early reduction' under the Carbon Neutrality Act [7, 14, 17]. Meanwhile, Gyeongsangbuk-do has shown positive results by exceeding its greenhouse gas reduction target by 121% [9, 13, 22], and Gyeonggi-do is expanding carbon-neutral volunteering in daily life by hosting the 'Carbon-Off Challenge Day' [8, 20]. The National Assembly Climate Change Forum is recruiting students for its university academy [1], and the Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs is holding an international symposium [11], demonstrating active climate action through education and cooperation.

sections.title: 🌱 South Korea Unveils Roadmap to Accelerate Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: The government has announced its 'Basic Plan for Renewable Energy,' aiming to supply 100 gigawatts (GW) of renewable energy capacity by 2030 and expand its share in power generation to 30% by 2035 [23, 25, 27, 28, 32, 33, 37, 39, 42, 44, 47, 51, 53]. This plan includes the construction of more than 10 'nuclear power plant-scale' super-large solar power complexes in the central region and areas bordering Gangwon Province [36, 41, 43, 53]. Furthermore, the Korea Water Resources Corporation (K-water) has achieved RE100 for the first time in Korea, expanding direct transactions of renewable energy [35], and Siheung City is recruiting applicants for renewable energy installations [10, 24, 30, 31, 38], demonstrating active participation from local governments and public institutions. An amendment to the 'Enforcement Decree of the Local Public Enterprises Act,' which lowers the investment threshold for local public corporations in solar and wind power, has been pre-announced for legislation [48], and the Ministry of Climate is striving to address the flexibility issues of renewable energy through the development of next-generation Energy Storage System (ESS) technology [49].

sections.title: 🔴 Severe Threats of Climate Change Facing the International Community
sections.content: The threats of climate change to ecosystems and societies worldwide are becoming increasingly clear. Warnings have been repeatedly reported that iconic seabird species are facing extinction due to climate change [59, 62, 63, 69, 76, 79, 84, 87, 89], and Colorado's famous peaches are threatened by fungal diseases and climate change [61]. Furthermore, scientists have discovered that global warming is accelerating 5,000 times faster than the rate at which rice can evolve [81], and is reducing oxygen levels in rivers worldwide [86]. In the UK, lifestyles are threatened by heatwaves, floods, and droughts, and a report warning that current buildings were 'built for a climate that no longer exists' has highlighted the urgent need for change [82, 94, 95].

sections.title: 🌍 Expansion of International Climate Discourse and Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: Internationally, discourse and adaptation efforts regarding climate change are continuously expanding. Some scientists have presented the view that worst-case climate scenarios like 'RCP8.5' are 'unrealistic,' sparking in-depth discussions on climate predictions [54, 57, 70, 98]. Oceans play a crucial role in combating climate change [58], and UCLA researchers have demonstrated nature's resilience by identifying oak populations that adapt well to climate change [55]. Meanwhile, positive changes at the local level are also observed, with some communities in conservative Utah transitioning from fossil fuel-based power to clean energy [78]. Research indicating that personal experience is important for climate action and that effective climate action can gain majority support through citizen deliberation [65, 67, 68] suggests the potential for global climate action.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has shown that while there are various domestic and international responses to the climate crisis, severe threats still coexist. This is a time that requires continuous attention and effort. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: The government's first 'Renewable Energy Master Plan' targets 100GW of installed capacity by 2030 and over 30% power generation share by 2035, promoting renewable energy as a 'future strategic industry'. Plans to address disadvantages like high costs are also included, with civic groups deeming the goals appropriate.

summary.phenomenon: According to the government's first 'Renewable Energy Master Plan' announced on the 19th, there are plans to construct over 10 'nuclear power plant-scale' solar complexes in the central region by 2030. This plan aims to achieve 100GW of renewable energy installed capacity by 2030 and over 30% of power generation share by 2035.
summary.cause: The government is pursuing this plan to foster renewable energy as a 'future strategic industry'.
summary.outlook: The government plans to address the disadvantages of renewable energy, such as high costs, and civic groups have evaluated the goals as appropriate.

articles.original_title: UK ‘built for climate that no longer exists’ and needs urgent changes to survive global heating, report warns
articles.description: Landmark report calls for widespread air conditioning and says UK temperatures forecast to exceed 40C by 2050British homes will need air conditioning to survive predicted levels of global heating, the government’s climate advisers have warned in a report, as measures such as drawing curtains, opening windows and growing trees for shade are not likely to be enough.Air conditioning should be installed in all care homes and hospitals within the next 10 years, and in all schools within 25 years, acc

summary.phenomenon: According to a report by the UK government's climate advisors, UK temperatures are forecast to exceed 40C by 2050. This implies that current UK homes are not adequately equipped to withstand predicted levels of global heating.
summary.outlook: The UK will need to install air conditioning in all care homes and hospitals within 10 years, and in all schools within 25 years, to prepare for predicted levels of global heating. Existing measures such as drawing curtains or opening windows are unlikely to be sufficient.

articles.original_description: Landmark report calls for widespread air conditioning and says UK temperatures forecast to exceed 40C by 2050British homes will need air conditioning to survive predicted levels of global heating, the government’s climate advisers have warned in a report, as measures such as drawing curtains, opening windows and growing trees for shade are not likely to be enough.Air conditioning should be installed in all care homes and hospitals within the next 10 years, and in all schools within 25 years, acc

articles.original_title: Britain must think like a hot country – otherwise inequalities will only grow
articles.description: The government must act to redress the unequal impact of climate change, or risk rising temperatures making disparities worseUK ‘built for climate that no longer exists’ and needs urgent changes to survive global heating, report warnsIt may not always feel like it, but Britons are going to have to get used to living in a hot country.Temperatures are already 1.4C above the historic norm, and heading for a 2C rise in the next two decades. This may not sound like much, but it will mean far higher t

summary.phenomenon: UK temperatures are already 1.4C above the historic norm and are expected to rise by 2C within the next two decades. This suggests that the UK was built for a climate that no longer exists.
summary.outlook: The UK government must act to redress the unequal impact of climate change, or risk rising temperatures exacerbating disparities. Britons will need to get used to living in a hotter country.

articles.original_title: ‘Foolish’ CSIRO job cuts will mean Australia unable to provide climate projections to global reports, scientists warn
articles.description: Exclusive: Science agency is planning to sack a third of the team working on the national climate model, sources sayFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastJob cuts at the national science agency mean Australia will no longer be able to submit climate projections to form part of global reports and will have significantly reduced ability to forecast future damage to the country, leading researchers have warned.Multiple sourc

summary.phenomenon: Australia's national science agency (CSIRO) is planning to dismiss one-third of the team working on the national climate model.
summary.outlook: Leading researchers have warned that these job cuts will prevent Australia from submitting climate projections for global reports and will significantly reduce its ability to forecast future damage to the country.
